fn3: One should not expect consistency in accounts of creation. In some accounts, Marichi was born from Brahma and Marichi’s son was Kashyapa, from whom all humans are descended through Manu. In other accounts, Brahma had seven sons born from his mental powers, with Vashishtha added to the list of six that is given in the text. These seven (or six) are prajapatis or rulers of the world. In other accounts, ten prajapatis were born from Brahma’s right thumb and one of these was Daksha. Daksha had several daughters, thirteen were married to Kashyapa and twentyseven were married to the moon or Chandra. Section 1 states that Daksha had seven sons and that Daksha was born from an ancient sage named Prachetas. But this is a rare reference. Daksha’s birth from Brahma is more common.
fn4: The world was being populated by offspring of the gods, and the demons are also descended from Daksha’s daughters. In fact, every living species is descended from Daksha’s daughters.
fn8: Tarkshya is also a name for both Garuda and Garuda’s elder brother Aruna. The text suggests that Garuda and Aruna had another brother named Tarkshya.
fn11: There is an inconsistency with what the text has just said about Prava’s progeny, the gandharvas and the apsaras.
fn13: Mythical beings with human bodies and heads of horses.
fn14: This is a little confusing, because there doesn’t seem to be any difference between kinnaras and kimpurushas.
fn15: These were the valakhilya sages, 60,000 in number. Generated from the creator’s body, they are the size of a thumb and precede the sun’s chariot.
fn17: Daksha. Prajapati is also one of Brahma’s names.
fn18: The daughters effectively become sons in one of two ways. Either, the daughter, since she is regarded as a son, returns to her father’s house after marriage, or the daughter’s son is adopted as a son by his maternal grandfather.
fn25: The word son doesn’t always mean son in a literal sense. It can also mean progeny.
fn29: There are twenty-seven lunar mansions (nakshatras) and krittika is the third, identiﬁed with the Pleiades. However, there are actually six stars in the Pleiades. Hence the use of the plural krittikas, and the krittikas reared Kumara, which is why he is known as Kartikeya.
fn31: Famous sage, preceptor of the gods.
fn32: Vivasvat’s son. Identiﬁed with Vishvakarma, architect of the gods.
fn38: A class of demi-gods; a semi-divine species, Kubera’s attendants.
fn41: That is, set her free from her pregnancy.
fn43: Ourva is another name for Chyavana. He was thus named because he was born through splitting open the thigh (uru).
fn45: Not to be confused with Richika’s son Jamadagni. Richika’s son is the more famous Jamadagni.
fn52: Monkeys with tails like those of cows, with dark bodies and red cheeks.
fn53: The cardinal points are guarded by elephants.
fn55: There were probably two Surasas, one who was Kadru’s daughter and another who was Krodha’s daughter.
fn56: A shyena is a hawk or falcon, Shyeni is the feminine. So Shyeni could have been married to Vinata’s son Aruna, though the text doesn’t make it clear if the two Arunas were the same.
fn63: Literally, those who are slaves to anger.
fn64: This is known as Dhanur Veda.
fn69: The eight vasus, sons of Manu, were reborn on earth as Shantanu’s sons.
fn73: A class of gods often described as ten in number. Their mother was Vishva.
fn78: Barbarians, non-Aryans, those who did not speak Sanskrit.
fn79: The god of rain. That is, Indra.
fn82: That is, Garuda. The speed was like that of Garuda.
fn84: The word used in the text is shatapada, one with six legs. This is a word for a bee.
fn88: A yati is speciﬁcally an ascetic.
fn89: The word used in the text is kaccha. This is usually the word used for a marsh. But a marsh does not seem right, given the context.
fn92: This is probably a reference to the Yajur Veda. The critical edition jumps from the Rig Veda to the Atharva Veda, at least by name. The Yajur Veda is not explicitly mentioned. Some regional variations mention the Yajur Veda explicitly and there are shlokas, missing in the critical edition, that mention the Sama Veda.
fn101: A nakshatra, that is, a star or constellation.
fn102: The wind-god. Vayu is another one of his names.
fn103: The god of love. Kama is another one of his names.
fn104: She is actually repeating Kanva’s words.
fn106: The word used is shakuna. In a general sense, this means bird. But in a speciﬁc sense, it means vultures or kites.
fn108: One of the eight forms of marriage. In this form, there are no ceremonies and no relatives are present. The bride and the groom willingly marry each other in what is a marriage of love.
fn110: It is not clear what this number ﬁve refers to, probably the last ﬁve of the eight mentioned forms.
fn111: With infantry, cavalry, elephants and chariots.
fn113: A wheel, the sign of a king.
fn114: Sarva is everything and damana is to subjugate.
fn115: The word used is yuvaraja, which can be translated as prince or young king. However, this appellation also signiﬁes the person becoming an heir apparent.
fn117: Literally, the city of the elephant and another name for Hastinapura, Duhshanta’s capital.
fn121: There are three kinds of sacriﬁcial ﬁres—ahavaniya, garhapatya and dakshina. The garhapatya ﬁre burns in the household, the ahavaniya ﬁre is the one into which sacriﬁcial offerings are placed and the dakshina ﬁre is the one that faces the south.
fn122: There were originally thirty or thirty-three gods —eleven in heaven, eleven in the sky and eleven on earth. Another way of getting the number is by adding twelve adityas, eleven rudras and ten vishvadevas.
fn124: That is, the gift of a pond.
fn126: A universal monarch or sovereign of the world. Sarvabhouma has the same meaning.
fn127: It is not clear what this means. A padma is a lotus. But this doesn’t quite ﬁt the requirement. Were one thousand lotuses given? A padma is also one trillion. In that case, one trillion of what is left unsaid. Perhaps a padma simply connotes a large number and a large number of cows were given.
fn129: One of six schools of philosophy, attributed to the sage Kapila.
fn130: The word for men is manava.
fn131: Manu is not the name of a single person. There were fourteen Manus, each presiding over the interval between a round of destruction and recreation. The ﬁrst Manu (Svayambhuva) produced the ﬁrst round of human beings. However, the present round of human beings is descended from the seventh Manu (Vaivasvata).
fn133: Robbers or dacoits. The word also means evil beings or demons that were enemies of gods and men. It can also mean outcasts or outcastes.
fn136: Brihaspati, the preceptor of the gods.
fn137: The demons. That is, the demons also had a priest for the same purpose.
fn138: Ushanas is another name for Shukra or Shukracharya, the preceptor of the demons. The text has the word kavya, which has been translated as wise. However, Kavya is also another name for Shukra and Kavya is the son of Kavi.
fn139: The knowledge of bringing the dead back to life.
fn141: Sacriﬁcial ﬁre kindled in the evening. Presumably, this was Kacha’s task.
fn143: The brighter half of the lunar month.
fn146: Indra. Literally, the performer of a hundred sacriﬁces.
fn147: Indra. Literally, the destroyer of cities.
fn149: Meaning that the forest or the wood was beautiful. Chitraratha was the king of the gandharvas.
fn150: The text actually has Devayani addressing Sharmishtha as her disciple. This is because Devayani’s father (Shukra) was the preceptor to Sharmishtha’s father (Vrishaparva).
fn151: One of Devayani’s companions or maids.
fn152: Before the quarrel, Devayani and Sharmishtha were friends.
fn153: There is a break in continuity in the critical edition. Other editions have a shloka where Shukra goes and tells Devayani what has occurred.
fn154: That of giving birth to a child.
fn157: This isn’t clear at all. There was a kingdom of Bhoja in the Vidarbha or Malwa region. But why should that be a curse? Or is this appellation a reference to people who were addicted to food (bhojana)?
fn158: Usually there is reference to three sacriﬁcial ﬁres—ahavaniya, garhapatya and dakshina. The other two sacred ﬁres are samya and avasadhya.
fn160: Clearly, not material prosperity, but riches obtained through righteousness.
fn161: The text should literally be translated as those born from sweat.
fn164: From an earlier reference, one knows that Ashtaka was a king. Yayati was his maternal grandfather.
fn165: The ﬁrst of the four yugas, also known as Satya Yuga.
fn166: The word used is shitikantha, which means white in the neck. This means a peacock, though some kind of vulture with a white neck is also possible.
fn167: That is, through the merits of one’s earlier deeds.
fn168: As enumerated in what immediately follows, the ﬁve sense organs and mind.
fn170: None of this is to be interpreted in a literal sense. For instance, the teeth are clean because he lives on pure food, the nails are clipped because he does not hurt anyone, he is always bathed and without dirt because his ac-
fn171: Pain and pleasure, happiness and unhappiness, and so on.
fn172: Cattle and other animals eat to survive, without making eating the end object.
fn173: The ascetic and the householder who has learning.
fn174: As mentioned earlier, Pratardana was also a king.
fn176: An arbuda is one hundred million.
fn178: Earth, water, energy, air and the sky.
fn179: The suggestion is that the son was actually Bharadvaja’s.
fn180: Fourfold armies include elephants, horses, chariots and infantry.
fn183: The word used is vishana, which means both horn (as in trumpet) and tusk.
fn186: Not to be confused with Parikshit, Arjuna’s grandson.
fn187: Not to be confused with the horse.
fn188: Designed to ensure conquest (jita) of the world (vishva).
fn189: The word prach means eastern or easterly.
fn191: The word used is bhastra, which is a leather bag or pouch, usually used for storing water.
fn193: The word shanta means to appease, calm, satisy, pacify, cure or alleviate.
fn196: Ceremony where the bride chooses her own groom.
fn197: Shulka is price, but this not a marriage where a bride price is paid. Virya means valour, so this is a marriage where the bride is married to the person who exhibits the most valour.
fn199: The word parikshina means diminished, decayed, thin. In this context, even extinct can be used.
fn200: There doesn’t seem to be much point in telling Janamejaya this. Perhaps the intention is to make the account complete.
fn201: A sacriﬁce where offerings of food and water were made.
fn202: Celestial, terrestrial and subterranean. The river Ganga ﬂows in all three regions.
fn203: As mentioned earlier, the word shanta means to appease, calm, satisy, pacify, cure or alleviate. With the same etymological root, earlier, a slightly different twist has been given to the name Shantanu, concerning Shantanu’s traits and not those of his father.
fn207: Ganga. Jahnavi is Ganga’s name because Ganga is Jahnu’s daughter.
fn212: Indra. Literally, one who sacks cities.
fn217: The maintenance of the sacred ﬁre.
fn218: The word three here accordingly refers to the ﬁrst three Vedas.
fn220: Indra, the performer of a hundred sacriﬁces.
fn223: The terrible or fearsome one, because of the vow he took. The word bhishma means terrible or fearssome.
fn224: The gandharva was also named Chitrangada.
fn226: That is, through a system of bride price being paid.
fn227: The eight forms of marriage actually are brahma, daiva, arsha, prajapatya, asura, gandharva, rakshasa and paishacha.
fn231: Soubha is the name of the kingdom. The king’s name is Shalva.
fn235: The king of the Haihayas was Kartavirya Arjuna.
fn236: That is, the person who married the mother, not the one who begot the son.
fn237: The critical edition has eliminated shlokas that tell us Dirghatama was born blind. Hence, the long period of darkness is left unexplained.
fn238: Dirghatama. One now knows that Dirghatama was blind.
fn239: This bull among men is Janamejaya. Although Bhishma is now telling the story to Satyavati, it is being retold by Vaishampayana. However, this reference to Janamejaya shouldn’t have been here, since it is Bhishma who is speaking.
fn241: The word vyasa means to divide or distribute.
fn242: Satyavati’s name. She was so called because she was dark.
fn243: Both Ambika and Ambalika, who were princesses from the kingdom of Kashi. Kousalya means a princess of Kosala.
fn245: The word pandu means pale or white.
fn247: The word ani means point, usually of a needle.
fn248: Animandavya was a child when he pierced the insect.
fn249: Krita Yuga or Satya Yuga, the ﬁrst of the four yugas and a time when dharma was at its best. The incidents of the Mahabharata occurred during Dvapara Yuga, the third of the yugas.
fn251: The word used is kripana. It can also be translated as miserly.
fn253: Because he was born of a Shudra woman.
fn255: Bhishma was speaking to Vidura in particular.
fn257: This is a reference to an incident when the gods refused to give Shiva (Hara) a share in the sacriﬁcial offerings. Shiva ripped out the god Bhaga’s eyes.
fn259: She would face a difﬁculty in having sons.
fn262: Once she was reared by Kuntibhoja, Pritha obtained the name Kunti.
fn264: Vasu means riches and the riches he was born with were the natural armour and earrings.
fn265: Indra. Indra was attempting to protect and help Arjuna.
fn266: A shakti is a spear or lance. But this one was divine.
fn267: Vikartana is the sun, so Vaikartana means the sun’s son. Karna means the ear.
fn268: Indra is Maghavan. His wife is Shachi. Since Shachi was Puloma’s daughter, she is Poulomi.
fn270: Hastinapura. Nagapura is another name for Hastinapura, the city of the elephant. The word naga means elephant.
fn272: There are two Kousalyas, Ambika and Ambalika. This one is Ambalika.
fn273: In this case, father means Bhishma.
fn276: Jayanta is the son of Indra and Shachi.
fn277: Purandara is Indra and Indra’s elephant is Airavata.
fn279: The word used is parashava. This means offspring born of a Brahmana father and a Shudra mother. While the word also means bastard, it couldn’t have been used in that sense. Because in that sense of bastard or mixed breed, Dhritarashtra and Pandu were no different.
fn280: There is a problem with the numbers, since there were only 100 pots.
fn281: This is a reference to Section 6, Adi-vamshavatarana Parva.
fn282: Meaning that the enemy was unprepared.
fn284: That is, a different tree for every night.
fn285: Not beg from more than seven families.
fn286: A name for Vidura, a man born from a Kshatriya woman and a Shudra man.
fn287: The four ashramas—brahmacharya, garhasthya, vanaprastha and sannyasa.
fn288: There is a problem of translation here. What has been translated as superior residents should literally be translated as residents of families. And inferior residents should literally be translated as residents of villages. This doesn’t quite seem right. Hence village is probably being used in an inferior and normative sense.
fn293: These are both heirs and relatives.
fn294: These are neither heirs, nor relatives. As listed, the number adds up to seven and not six. Nor are the differences very clear. For instance, purchased/bought or gifted/presented occur in both categories. In the ﬁrst list, the word for purchased is parikrita. In the second list, the word for purchased is krita. The text doesn’t enable us to deduce the difference and is this also the case with gifted/presented.
fn303: Agni, or ﬁre. Speciﬁcally havya is sacriﬁcial offerings and vahana is the one who bears. Havyavahana is therefore the sacriﬁcial ﬁre into which offerings are placed.
fn306: Bibhatsu is one of Arjuna’s names and means the terrible one.
fn307: Meaning that she was superior in birth to Kunti.
fn308: By obtaining more sons than Kunti.
fn310: That is, he did not succumb to desire with Kunti.
fn312: There were parts of the bodies that had not been burnt. Having said this, there is some contradiction about when the bodies were burnt.
fn313: The word used is vardhaman, which means increasing. This has been translated as the chief gate. But it is perfectly possible for this to have been the proper name for the gate.
fn318: This is yet another name for Hastinapura. The word varana means elephant.
fn321: Kunti’s son. In this case, Bhima.
fn324: The word used is sarathi, which usually means charioteer, but also means companion. Charioteer doesn’t make any sense here, companion makes more sense. The companions are Bhima’s brothers.
fn325: Vidura. It could also mean Yuyutsu.
fn326: There is a break of continuity in the critical edition and Kripa appears out of the blue. In other editions, we are told that Dhritarashtra wanted to ﬁnd a teacher for the princes, since they were getting out of control. Kripa, descended from Goutama, was chosen to teach the art of ﬁghting.
fn327: The word shara means arrow and Sharadvat is one with arrows.
fn328: The science or knowledge of weapons.
fn329: The word kripa means compassion. One of the twins was a boy and he was named Kripa. The other one was a girl and she was named Kripi.
fn331: There are four upavedas or minor vedas, of which, dhanurveda is one. Each upaveda is attached to one of the main vedas. The other three upavedas are ayurveda (medicine), gandharvaveda (music) and sthapatyaveda (architecture).
fn332: There is a break in continuity in the critical edition. Bharadvaja had gone to the Ganga for a bath before making the offerings into the ﬁre.
fn333: Drona. This is usually a wooden vessel.
fn335: Ashva means horse and sthaman means strength or stamina.
fn339: The word used is shrotriya, which can also be translated as a learned Brahmana.
fn342: The word used is vita. I have translated this as ball. But there is no evidence to suggest that this was spherical. In all probability, this was a short piece of wood struck with another long piece of wood, something like a bat. Guli-danda may be the closest approximation.
fn343: The word used is ishika and means reed. In recounting this particular anecdote, renderings invariably interpret this as reed or grass. However, ishika also means arrow and Drona could have used arrows instead of reeds. There is nothing in the text to counter the arrow interpretation.
fn345: Implying weapons that were held in the hand.
fn346: Hunter or ﬁsherman. Usually, described as dwelling in the mountains and forests.
fn347: The text uses the word shabdabheda, which is the art of shooting with the aid of sound alone, without seeing the object. Earlier, there was no suggestion that Ekalavya had not seen the dog when he was shooting the arrows. From the use of this word here, it seems that he had not seen the dog and had only heard it bark.
fn350: A maharatha is a warrior who can take on 10,000 warriors single-handed. An atiratha can take on 60,000.
fn351: Kunti’s son. In this case, Yudhishthira.
